text.title: Accessibility-First Design — Thresholds and Rules
text.summary: Concrete, testable thresholds for color contrast, touch targets, motion, focus states, and semantic structure that must be resolved at design time.

rule.statement: Body text must meet a 4.5:1 contrast ratio against its background (WCAG AA). Large text (18pt+ or 14pt bold) requires 3:1. UI components and focus indicators require 3:1.
rule.rationale: Contrast ratios below these thresholds make text unreadable for low-vision users and in high-ambient-light environments.

rule.statement: Never use color as the sole indicator of meaning (e.g., red = error). Always pair color with a text label, icon, or pattern.
rule.rationale: Approximately 8% of males have color vision deficiency; color-only signals are invisible to them.

rule.statement: Test designs with a color blindness simulator (e.g., Stark plugin) for deuteranopia, protanopia, and achromatopsia before developer handoff.
rule.rationale: Simulation catches color-only patterns that pass contrast ratio checks but still fail color-blind users.

rule.statement: Touch targets must be at minimum 24×24px (WCAG 2.2 AA). Use 44×44px as the production target; 48×48dp for Material Design contexts.
rule.rationale: Targets below 24px are consistently missed by users with motor impairments and elderly users with reduced dexterity.

rule.statement: Maintain at least 8px spacing between adjacent interactive elements to prevent mis-taps.
rule.rationale: Dense interactive element layouts produce high error rates for users with tremors or limited fine motor control.

rule.statement: Design visible focus indicators for every interactive element with a minimum 3:1 contrast ratio against adjacent colors. Never remove the default browser focus ring without a replacement.
rule.rationale: Keyboard-only users and switch control users navigate entirely via focus indicators; invisible focus makes the interface unusable.

rule.statement: Plan and annotate the keyboard focus order in design specs. Logical tab order must follow reading order; modal dialogs must trap focus while open and return focus to the trigger on close.
rule.rationale: An unspecified focus order results in developer-guessed tab sequences that are often illogical or inaccessible.

rule.statement: All animations must be pausable and must respect the prefers-reduced-motion media query. No auto-playing video with sound. No flashing content above 3 flashes per second.
rule.rationale: Motion triggers vestibular disorders in some users; flashing above 3 Hz can induce photosensitive seizures.

rule.statement: Remove time limits from interactive flows, or make them adjustable and extendable with at least 20 seconds warning.
rule.rationale: Fixed time limits exclude slow readers, users with cognitive disabilities, and screen reader users who process content sequentially.

rule.statement: Specify heading levels (H1 → H2 → H3) in design annotations. Never skip a heading level. Every page must have exactly one H1.
rule.rationale: Screen reader users navigate by heading; a broken hierarchy makes content structure incomprehensible.

rule.statement: All form inputs must have visible labels connected via for/id. Placeholder text is not a label substitute — it disappears on input and has insufficient contrast.
rule.rationale: Placeholder-only labels fail blind users who cannot see the placeholder and sighted users once they start typing.

rule.statement: Include in design handoff: heading levels, image alt text, link text (no "click here"), focus order, ARIA labels for custom components, error message copy, loading state announcements.
rule.rationale: Developers cannot infer accessibility semantics from visual designs; explicit annotation is required for correct implementation.
